content.text: After chunking and embedding, you have a list of dense float vectors — one per chunk. An index is a data structure that makes it fast to find the nearest vectors to a query vector without comparing every stored vector. This is the Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) search problem. Without an index, finding the 5 closest vectors out of 500,000 requires computing 500,000 cosine distances; with HNSW this becomes a graph traversal over roughly log(N) nodes. The index also stores the original text and metadata for each vector so the retriever can return the actual chunks, not just distances.

Four index strategies cover most use cases. HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World) builds a layered proximity graph; it offers the best recall-to-latency ratio and is the default in most managed stores. IVF (Inverted File) clusters vectors into Voronoi cells and only searches the nearest cells at query time; it is tunable but requires a training pass. Flat (brute-force) computes exact distances to every vector — perfect recall, only practical below 100,000 documents. Product Quantization and Scalar Quantization compress each vector to 4–10× smaller representations; they trade a small recall loss for dramatically lower RAM usage, important at hundreds of millions of vectors.
Building and Querying a Vector Store

Chroma is the standard local vector store for development and small production deployments. It uses HNSW internally, persists to disk automatically, and supports the full LangChain retriever interface. The example below covers the full lifecycle: building from documents, querying by similarity, querying with scores, and reloading an existing index.

# Building and querying a vector store with Chroma (local HNSW index)
# Chroma stores vectors + metadata in a local directory — no external service needed.

from langchain_community.vectorstores import Chroma
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
from langchain_core.documents import Document

embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")

# --- Build index from chunks ---
chunks = [
    Document(page_content="Refunds are processed within 30 days.", metadata={"source": "policy.txt", "section": "refunds"}),
    Document(page_content="Enterprise plan includes SLA and priority support.", metadata={"source": "pricing.txt", "section": "enterprise"}),
    Document(page_content="Free plan is limited to 1 user and 5 projects.", metadata={"source": "pricing.txt", "section": "free"}),
]

vectorstore = Chroma.from_documents(
    documents=chunks,
    embedding=embeddings,
    persist_directory="./chroma_db",
    collection_name="kb",
)
print(f"Indexed {vectorstore._collection.count()} chunks")

# --- Query ---
results = vectorstore.similarity_search("what does enterprise plan include?", k=3)
for doc in results:
    print(f"[{doc.metadata['source']}] {doc.page_content}")

# --- Similarity search with scores (cosine distance: lower = more similar) ---
scored = vectorstore.similarity_search_with_score("refund timeline", k=2)
for doc, score in scored:
    print(f"  score={score:.4f} | {doc.page_content}")

# --- Load existing index (subsequent runs) ---
loaded_store = Chroma(
    persist_directory="./chroma_db",
    embedding_function=embeddings,
    collection_name="kb",
)
print(f"Loaded {loaded_store._collection.count()} chunks from disk")

Most RAG corpora are heterogeneous — they contain documents from different sources, departments, time periods, or access levels. Metadata filters restrict ANN search to a predefined subset before ranking by similarity. Filtering happens before vector comparison in most stores (pre-filtering), which means incorrect metadata at index time produces silent retrieval failures — the query matches no documents even though relevant text exists. The filter syntax follows MongoDB conventions and is consistent across Chroma, Pinecone, and pgvector.

# Metadata filtering: restrict vector search to a subset of the index
# Without filtering, ANN search scans all vectors — filters pre-eliminate candidates.
# Chroma uses MongoDB-style operators: $eq, $ne, $gt, $gte, $lt, $lte, $in, $nin, $and, $or.

from langchain_community.vectorstores import Chroma
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
from langchain_core.documents import Document

embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")
vectorstore = Chroma(persist_directory="./chroma_db", embedding_function=embeddings)

# Exact match: only search chunks from a specific source file
results = vectorstore.similarity_search(
    "pricing details",
    k=4,
    filter={"source": "pricing.txt"},
)

# $in operator: search across multiple sources
results = vectorstore.similarity_search(
    "compliance requirements",
    k=4,
    filter={"source": {"$in": ["policy.txt", "compliance.txt"]}},
)

# Range filter: only chunks from 2024 or later
results = vectorstore.similarity_search(
    "data retention rules",
    k=4,
    filter={"year": {"$gte": 2024}},
)

# Compound: source must be policy AND year >= 2024
results = vectorstore.similarity_search(
    "security audit requirements",
    k=4,
    filter={
        "$and": [
            {"source": {"$eq": "security_policy.txt"}},
            {"year": {"$gte": 2024}},
        ]
    },
)

# Access control: per-user filtering
def search_for_user(query: str, user_dept: str, clearance_level: int, k: int = 4):
    """Only return chunks the user is allowed to see."""
    return vectorstore.similarity_search(
        query,
        k=k,
        filter={
            "$and": [
                {"department": {"$in": [user_dept, "all"]}},
                {"clearance": {"$lte": clearance_level}},
            ]
        },
    )

hr_results = search_for_user("vacation policy", user_dept="HR", clearance_level=2)

Pinecone is a fully managed vector database that handles index sharding, replication, and scaling transparently. The serverless tier requires no capacity planning — you pay per query and per vector stored. Namespaces provide per-tenant logical isolation within a single index, which is the correct multi-tenant pattern: one index, one namespace per customer. Mixing tenants in the same namespace and filtering by tenant ID is an anti-pattern — it adds unnecessary metadata to every chunk and risks cross-tenant bleed if the filter is accidentally omitted.

# Pinecone: managed vector database for production scale
# Serverless Pinecone handles index management, sharding, and replication automatically.
# Namespaces provide logical isolation — useful for multi-tenant applications.

import pinecone
from langchain_pinecone import PineconeVectorStore
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
from langchain_core.documents import Document

pc = pinecone.Pinecone(api_key="PINECONE_API_KEY")

# Create index once — dimension must match embedding model output
INDEX_NAME = "knowledge-base"
if INDEX_NAME not in [idx.name for idx in pc.list_indexes()]:
    pc.create_index(
        name=INDEX_NAME,
        dimension=1536,       # text-embedding-3-small
        metric="cosine",
        spec=pinecone.ServerlessSpec(cloud="aws", region="us-east-1"),
    )

embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")

# Each tenant gets its own namespace — vectors are isolated, no cross-tenant bleed
def get_store(tenant_id: str) -> PineconeVectorStore:
    return PineconeVectorStore(
        index_name=INDEX_NAME,
        embedding=embeddings,
        namespace=f"tenant_{tenant_id}",
    )

# Ingest for tenant A
store_a = get_store("acme_corp")
store_a.add_documents([
    Document(page_content="Acme refund policy: 60 days.", metadata={"source": "policy"}),
])

# Query for tenant A — cannot see tenant B's data
results = store_a.similarity_search("refund window", k=4)

# Batch upsert for large initial loads
# Pinecone recommends batches of 100 vectors at ~2-3 MB each
from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter
from langchain_community.document_loaders import TextLoader
from pathlib import Path

def ingest_files(file_paths: list[str], tenant_id: str):
    store = get_store(tenant_id)
    splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(chunk_size=512, chunk_overlap=64)
    for path in file_paths:
        loader = TextLoader(path)
        raw = loader.load()
        chunks = splitter.split_documents(raw)
        # add_documents handles batching internally
        store.add_documents(chunks)
        print(f"Indexed {len(chunks)} chunks from {path}")

pgvector extends PostgreSQL with a vector column type and HNSW / IVF index support. It is the right choice when your application already runs Postgres and you need semantic search alongside relational queries — for example, finding the most semantically similar support tickets filed by users in a specific account tier. Joining on the same database avoids a round-trip to a separate vector service. For corpora above 500,000 rows, you must CREATE an explicit HNSW index; without it, PostgreSQL performs exact brute-force search, which is too slow for interactive queries.

# pgvector: add ANN search to an existing PostgreSQL database
# Use when you need semantic search AND relational joins in the same query.
# pgvector supports three index types: ivfflat (IVF), hnsw, and none (exact/flat).

from langchain_postgres import PGVector
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
from langchain_core.documents import Document

CONNECTION = "postgresql+psycopg://user:password@localhost:5432/rag_db"
embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")

# Create (or connect to) a collection — creates the table and index automatically
store = PGVector(
    embeddings=embeddings,
    collection_name="documents",
    connection=CONNECTION,
    use_jsonb=True,   # store metadata in JSONB for fast $-filter queries
)

# Ingest
store.add_documents([
    Document(
        page_content="Q4 revenue grew 18% year-over-year.",
        metadata={"source": "earnings_2024.pdf", "quarter": "Q4", "year": 2024},
    ),
    Document(
        page_content="Operating costs increased due to headcount growth.",
        metadata={"source": "earnings_2024.pdf", "quarter": "Q4", "year": 2024},
    ),
])

# Semantic search with JSONB metadata filter
results = store.similarity_search(
    "revenue growth last year",
    k=4,
    filter={"year": 2024},
)

# pgvector index creation (run once for large tables — significant performance gain)
# Execute via psycopg / raw SQL:
#
#   CREATE INDEX ON langchain_pg_embedding
#   USING hnsw (embedding vector_cosine_ops)
#   WITH (m = 16, ef_construction = 64);
#
# hnsw is recommended for < 1M rows; ivfflat for larger tables.
# After creating the index, ANALYZE the table so the planner uses it.
print("pgvector store ready.")

A single source document can be represented as multiple vectors in the index. The most effective pattern is indexing summaries for retrieval but returning the full parent document for generation. Summaries are short and semantically dense — they match broad queries better than raw chunks. The full parent document gives the LLM more context than a 512-token chunk. LangChain's MultiVectorRetriever implements this: store summaries in the vector index with a parent ID, store full documents in a separate key-value docstore, and let the retriever fetch the parent after matching on the summary.

# Multi-vector indexing: index multiple representations of the same source document
# A single parent document can be indexed as: summaries (for recall) + small chunks (for precision).
# The retriever returns the full parent document after matching on the smaller/summary vectors.

from langchain.storage import InMemoryByteStore
from langchain.retrievers import MultiVectorRetriever
from langchain_community.vectorstores import Chroma
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings, ChatOpenAI
from langchain_core.documents import Document
from langchain_core.output_parsers import StrOutputParser
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
import uuid

embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")
vectorstore = Chroma(collection_name="summaries", embedding_function=embeddings)

# The docstore holds the full parent documents by ID
docstore = InMemoryByteStore()

retriever = MultiVectorRetriever(
    vectorstore=vectorstore,
    byte_store=docstore,
    id_key="doc_id",
    search_kwargs={"k": 4},
)

# Parent documents to index
parent_docs = [
    Document(
        page_content="The enterprise plan includes: unlimited users, SSO, SOC 2 audit logs, dedicated account manager, 99.9% SLA, and 24/7 priority support. Annual billing only. Custom pricing for > 1000 seats.",
        metadata={"source": "pricing.txt"},
    ),
    Document(
        page_content="Data is retained for 7 years per GDPR Article 5(1)(e). Customers may request deletion via the privacy portal. Deletion is completed within 30 days. Backups are purged within 90 days of the deletion request.",
        metadata={"source": "privacy.txt"},
    ),
]

# Generate summaries for each parent document
summarize_chain = (
    ChatPromptTemplate.from_template("Summarize this document in 1 sentence: {doc}")
    | ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini", temperature=0)
    | StrOutputParser()
)

doc_ids = [str(uuid.uuid4()) for _ in parent_docs]
summaries = summarize_chain.batch([{"doc": d.page_content} for d in parent_docs])

# Index the summaries with their parent ID as metadata
summary_docs = [
    Document(page_content=s, metadata={"doc_id": doc_id})
    for s, doc_id in zip(summaries, doc_ids)
]
retriever.vectorstore.add_documents(summary_docs)

# Store full parent docs in the byte store keyed by ID
import pickle
retriever.docstore.mset([(doc_id, pickle.dumps(doc)) for doc_id, doc in zip(doc_ids, parent_docs)])

# Query: matches on summaries, returns full parent documents
results = retriever.invoke("what support is included in enterprise?")
for doc in results:
    print(f"FULL DOC: {doc.page_content[:120]}...")

The retriever wraps a vector store and exposes a standard interface for LCEL chains. Three retrieval modes are worth knowing. Plain similarity returns the top-k closest vectors — the default. Score threshold adds a minimum similarity cutoff that silently drops results below the threshold, preventing low-quality matches when the query is outside the index's domain. MMR (Maximal Marginal Relevance) reranks candidates to penalize near-duplicate results, which is important when the corpus contains many slightly-different versions of the same text (repeated boilerplate, copied policy language across subsidiaries).

# Configuring retrievers: turn a vector store into a retriever with search options
# A retriever wraps a vector store and exposes a standard .invoke(query) interface
# compatible with LCEL chains. The key parameters are k, score_threshold, and search_type.

from langchain_community.vectorstores import Chroma
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings

embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")
vectorstore = Chroma(persist_directory="./chroma_db", embedding_function=embeddings)

# --- Basic retriever: return top-k by cosine similarity ---
retriever_k = vectorstore.as_retriever(search_kwargs={"k": 6})

# --- Score threshold: only return chunks above a minimum similarity ---
# Prevents low-quality matches from reaching the LLM when the query is out-of-domain.
retriever_threshold = vectorstore.as_retriever(
    search_type="similarity_score_threshold",
    search_kwargs={"score_threshold": 0.75, "k": 6},
)

# --- MMR (Maximal Marginal Relevance): balance relevance + diversity ---
# MMR penalizes redundant results — useful when many chunks are near-duplicates
# (e.g., repeated boilerplate across multiple PDF pages).
retriever_mmr = vectorstore.as_retriever(
    search_type="mmr",
    search_kwargs={
        "k": 6,           # number of results to return
        "fetch_k": 20,    # candidates to consider before MMR re-ranking
        "lambda_mult": 0.7,   # 1.0 = pure relevance, 0.0 = maximum diversity
    },
)

# --- Using a retriever in an LCEL chain ---
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from langchain_core.output_parsers import StrOutputParser
from langchain_core.runnables import RunnableParallel, RunnablePassthrough

def format_docs(docs):
    return "\n\n".join(d.page_content for d in docs)

prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_template(
    "Answer using only the context below.\n\nContext:\n{context}\n\nQuestion: {question}"
)
llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini", temperature=0)

rag_chain = (
    RunnableParallel(context=retriever_mmr | format_docs, question=RunnablePassthrough())
    | prompt
    | llm
    | StrOutputParser()
)

answer = rag_chain.invoke("What data deletion rights do users have?")
print(answer)

An index converts a flat list of float vectors into a structure that supports millisecond ANN search. HNSW is the default for most corpora. Metadata stored alongside each vector enables pre-filtering that restricts search to the relevant subset before ranking. Chroma is the standard local option; Pinecone handles production scale with zero operational overhead; pgvector extends an existing Postgres database with semantic search. Choose based on corpus size, existing infrastructure, and whether you need relational joins alongside semantic queries.
HNSW gives the best recall/latency ratio — use it by default for up to ~10M vectors.
Metadata filters restrict ANN search to a relevant subset; incorrect metadata causes silent retrieval failures.
Chroma → Pinecone → pgvector: choose by ops burden and infrastructure fit, not by feature set.
Multi-tenant corpora should use namespaces (Pinecone) or separate collections — never tenant-ID filters on a shared namespace.
